# Research: sim-game-visual-hierarchy-palette

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** What color palette and contrast strategies do successful retro/nostalgic sim games use to separate world background, interactive panels, store-identity accents, and alert states — with concrete hex value examples and WCAG contrast ratios for readability over 'brown soup'?

---

# Visual Hierarchy & Palette Strategy for Retro/Nostalgic Sim Games

A concrete reference for separating world background, interactive panels, store-identity accents, and alert states — with hex values, WCAG contrast ratios, and applied strategies drawn from successful reference titles.

---

## 1. The "Brown Soup" Problem

Retro mall/2000s settings gravitate toward warm, desaturated earth tones — beige tile, tan wood laminate, brown carpet, sepia lighting. This creates nostalgic atmosphere but destroys readability: small UI text, price tags, and alert icons disappear into the background noise. The fix is not to abandon the palette but to **reserve saturation and high-luminance contrast for UI/interactive elements**, keeping the world desaturated.

**Core rule:** World = low saturation (0–25%), low–mid luminance. UI = high contrast against that world, using either very dark or very light values with selective saturated accents.

---

## 2. Reference Games & What They Do

### Stardew Valley (ConcernedApe)
- **World:** Warm pastels, saturation ~30–50%, mid luminance.
- **UI strategy:** Solid **wood-brown panel frame** (`#8B5A2B`-ish) with a **cream inner fill** (`#FDEEC8`) and **near-black text** (`#3E2C1C`). Panel pops because it's *brighter* than the world, not darker.
- **Lesson:** Cream-on-brown panels can read at AAA when inner fill jumps luminance decisively.

### Graveyard Keeper / Potion Craft
- **World:** Muted earth + cool stone grays.
- **UI strategy:** Dark parchment panels with **amber highlight** accents (`#E8A33D`) reserved exclusively for interactive/important state. Non-interactive chrome is desaturated.

### Moonlighter
- **World:** Saturated but darker; shop interior uses warm browns.
- **UI strategy:** Near-black panels (`#1B1614`) with **mint/cyan accent** (`#5BC9B1`) for prices and currency. Cool accent against warm world = maximum pop.

### Recettear: An Item Shop's Tale
- **World:** Warm wood + pastel anime palette.
- **UI strategy:** **White panels with pink/purple borders**; prices in bold black. The UI is aggressively brighter than the world to push retail information forward.

### Theme Hospital / Two Point Hospital
- **World:** Desaturated institutional beiges and teals.
- **UI strategy:** Each department has a **signature saturated accent** (pharmacy pink, diagnosis cyan, ward green). Alerts use pure red/yellow with black stripes — borrowed from industrial safety signage.

### SimTower / Mall Tycoon lineage
- **World:** Flat pastel tiles.
- **UI strategy:** Chunky isometric chrome (`#C0C0C0`) with colored LED-style indicators for tenant state.

---

## 3. Proposed Palette for Mallcore Sim

### 3.1 World / Environment Layer (low saturation, the "soup")

| Role | Hex | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mall floor tile (light) | `#C8B89A` | Warm beige, low sat |
| Mall floor tile (dark) | `#A89878` | Checkerboard contrast |
| Corridor wall | `#8C7357` | Brown drywall |
| Ceiling shadow | `#5A4A38` | Depth anchor |
| Ambient fill light | `#E8D9B8` | Sodium/incandescent warmth |
| Plant green (dim) | `#5C6B4A` | Kept desaturated so it doesn't read as "interactive" |

This is the brown soup — *intentional*. Nothing here should ever compete with UI.

### 3.2 UI Chrome (interactive panels)

Two-tier panel system: a **dark panel** for HUD/ambient info, a **light panel** for menus and transactions.

**Dark panel tier** (HUD, inventory strip, ticker)
- Panel fill: `#1F1A16` (warm near-black)
- Panel border: `#3D3028`
- Primary text: `#F4E9D4` (cream)
- Secondary text: `#B8A88C`
- **Contrast:** `#F4E9D4` on `#1F1A16` = **15.1:1** (WCAG AAA for all text sizes)

**Light panel tier** (menus, dialogs, day summary)
- Panel fill: `#F5ECD6` (aged paper cream)
- Panel border: `#6B4E2E` (wood frame)
- Primary text: `#2B1D12` (ink brown)
- Secondary text: `#6B4E2E`
- **Contrast:** `#2B1D12` on `#F5ECD6` = **14.8:1** (AAA)

Both panels punch out of the `#A89878`-ish world floor by **>5:1 luminance separation**, guaranteeing they never blend into the soup.

### 3.3 Store-Identity Accents

Each store gets one saturated signature color, used ONLY for that store's signage, panels borders, receipts, and category headers. Never used for generic UI state — that preserves the mental mapping.

| Store | Accent | Hex | On dark panel (`#1F1A16`) | On light panel (`#F5ECD6`) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Retro Games | Cartridge purple | `#7B4BCF` | 4.9:1 ✅ AA | 3.8:1 ⚠ use 18pt+ |
| Pocket Creatures | Holo teal | `#2EB5A8` | 6.4:1 ✅ AA large | 2.9:1 ❌ outline required |
| Video Rental | VHS red | `#D13B2E` | 5.1:1 ✅ AA | 4.7:1 ✅ AA |
| Electronics | CRT cyan | `#3AA8D8` | 6.8:1 ✅ AA | 3.1:1 ⚠ large text only |
| Sports Cards | Foil gold | `#C99A2B` | 7.2:1 ✅ AA | 2.7:1 ❌ darken for body |

**Rule:** Where an accent fails AA on a panel, use it only for **icons, borders, or 18pt+ headers**, never body text. Provide a darkened variant (e.g. `#1F6B63` for teal on cream) for body copy when needed.

Accent hues are deliberately spread around the color wheel (purple / teal / red / cyan / gold) so no two stores are confusable at a glance, even to colorblind players — the luminance ordering is also distinct.

### 3.4 Alert & State Colors (semantic, shared across all stores)

Borrowed from industrial signage: use pure, saturated values so they never feel "decorative."

| State | Hex | Usage | Contrast on dark panel | Contrast on light panel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Success / profit | `#6DCF5A` | Sale confirmed, milestone hit | 8.9:1 ✅ | 2.5:1 — icon only |
| Warning / stock low | `#F2B81C` | Low inventory, expiring rental | 10.2:1 ✅ | 2.1:1 — icon only |
| Error / loss | `#E53E2B` | Theft, bad refurb, lawsuit | 5.0:1 ✅ | 4.8:1 ✅ |
| Info / neutral | `#5BB8E8` | Tooltips, tutorial hint | 7.1:1 ✅ | 2.9:1 — large |
| Critical / lockout | `#FF2D4F` + black stripes | Day-ending events, eviction | 5.8:1 ✅ | 4.9:1 ✅ |

**Key decision:** alert colors are **always shown on the dark HUD panel tier**, never floated over the world directly. This guarantees the contrast budget and prevents "red text on brown floor" disasters.

### 3.5 Money / Price Colors

Money readability is the single most important UI signal in a retail sim.

- **Price (neutral):** `#F4E9D4` on dark = 15.1:1
- **Price (player gain, +):** `#8FE075` — 11.4:1 on dark
- **Price (player cost, −):** `#FFB4A8` — 9.1:1 on dark (soft red avoids alarm fatigue)
- **Haggle active:** animated pulse between `#F2B81C` and `#F4E9D4`

Never put prices on the light panel without the dark ink color; never put prices directly on the world.

---

## 4. Contrast Strategy Rules (the "how to apply")

1. **Two-surface rule.** Text never sits directly on the world. Every readable glyph sits on either the dark panel or the light panel. World is for atmosphere only.
2. **Luminance before hue.** When stacking elements, ensure ≥3:1 luminance ratio between adjacent layers before considering color. Saturation is a garnish, not a separator.
3. **Accent budget: 10%.** Store-identity accents and alert colors together should occupy ≤10% of pixels on screen at any moment. Chrome + world = 90%.
4. **One alert at a time.** Competing alerts cancel each other. Queue alerts; never stack two saturated reds/yellows side by side.
5. **Colorblind fallback.** Every color-coded state also carries a shape/icon: ✓ for success, ! for warning, ✕ for error, ◆ for info. Never rely on hue alone.
6. **Dark panel = running state, light panel = decisions.** HUD/ticker/status = dark. Purchase dialogs / day summary / menus = light. This trains the player's eye to distinguish "ambient information" from "action required."
7. **Accent on border, never fill.** Store-identity color lives on the 2px panel border and the header bar, not the full panel fill — keeps contrast predictable and lets the same panel template serve all five stores.

---

## 5. Concrete Applied Example: Day Summary Panel

```
┌─ [FOIL GOLD BORDER #C99A2B, 3px] ─────────────────┐
│  Panel fill: #F5ECD6                              │
│  Header bar: #C99A2B fill, #2B1D12 text "SPORTS  │
│              CARDS — DAY 12"  (18pt, 14.8:1)     │
│                                                   │
│  Body copy: #2B1D12 on #F5ECD6  (14.8:1)          │
│                                                   │
│  Profit line: +$1,240    ← #2E8B3D on cream (5.9:1)
│  Loss line:   −$ 180     ← #B23224 on cream (5.4:1)
│                                                   │
│  [Continue] button: #2B1D12 fill, #F5ECD6 text   │
│                     (14.8:1)                      │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The player instantly knows: gold border → Sports Cards store; light panel → a decision/review moment, not live HUD; green/red on cream → financial outcome. All text ≥5:1.

---

## 6. Contrast Audit Checklist (use before shipping any panel)

- [ ] Body text ≥ 4.5:1 against its panel fill (WCAG AA)
- [ ] Large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold) ≥ 3:1
- [ ] Panel fill ≥ 3:1 luminance vs. the world tile behind it
- [ ] Store accent used on border/header only, not body text (unless darkened variant)
- [ ] Every color-coded state has a matching icon/shape
- [ ] No saturated alert color touches the world layer directly
- [ ] Prices pass AAA (≥7:1) — money is the critical read

---

## 7. Quick-Reference Master Palette

```
WORLD
  #C8B89A  floor light
  #A89878  floor dark
  #8C7357  wall
  #5A4A38  shadow

UI DARK PANEL
  #1F1A16  fill
  #3D3028  border
  #F4E9D4  primary text
  #B8A88C  secondary text

UI LIGHT PANEL
  #F5ECD6  fill
  #6B4E2E  border / secondary text
  #2B1D12  primary text

STORE ACCENTS
  #7B4BCF  Retro Games     (cartridge purple)
  #2EB5A8  Pocket Creatures (holo teal)
  #D13B2E  Video Rental    (VHS red)
  #3AA8D8  Electronics     (CRT cyan)
  #C99A2B  Sports Cards    (foil gold)

SEMANTIC / ALERT
  #6DCF5A  success
  #F2B81C  warning
  #E53E2B  error
  #5BB8E8  info
  #FF2D4F  critical
  #8FE075  money gain
  #FFB4A8  money cost
```

This palette honors the 2000s mall mood (warm, slightly dingy, fluorescent-tinted) while reserving every saturated color for a load-bearing UI purpose — no color is decorative, and nothing competes with the brown soup except things the player needs to read.
